name: MLton
summary: Cejtin, Jagannathan, Weeks et al. (1998+): the canonical whole-program optimising compiler for Standard ML. Defunctionalisation + monomorphisation + contification across ~20 SSA passes. C-competitive performance on numeric SML; HOL4 production scale. No separate compilation; minutes-scale compile times.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: **MLton** (Cejtin, Jagannathan, Weeks et al., 1998+) is the canonical whole-program optimising compiler for Standard ML and the most mature production-grade whole-program functional-language compiler in continuous use. It sits between Stalin and Virgil in the whole-program lineage: more aggressive than Virgil's reachability-first compilation (heavy monomorphisation, full defunctionalisation), but less radical than Stalin's flow-sensitive type inference (MLton starts from already-typed SML, so it skips the type-inference cost Stalin pays).

sections.title: Architectural pillars
sections.content:
- **Whole-program defunctionalisation**: every function value is replaced with a tagged sum of all syntactically-occurring lambdas in the program, plus a global `apply` dispatcher. Eliminates closures entirely in favour of first-order code that C-style optimisers can handle. Roc's lambda sets are the modern descendant of this design.
- **Aggressive monomorphisation**: every polymorphic SML function is specialised at every used type instantiation. Combined with defunctionalisation, the post-compilation SSA has no polymorphism, no closures, and no higher-order functions left.
- **Contification**: turning function calls into local jumps where the call has only one return point. This is a key MLton-specific optimisation — Cejtin, Jagannathan, Weeks's 1998 contification analysis identifies functions that can be "lowered" into the calling control-flow graph as basic blocks, eliminating entire call/return overhead and unlocking subsequent CFG-level optimisations.
- **SSA-based optimiser pipeline** with ~20 distinct passes: contification, inlining, loop-invariant code motion, common subexpression elimination, useless-variable elimination, redundant-tuple elimination, and **ref-flattening** (turning `ref` cells into mutable struct fields when the alias graph permits).
- **Native code generation** via either an in-tree backend (x86, AMD64, ARM64) or LLVM. Performance is competitive with hand-written C on numeric kernels.

sections.title: Production and trade-offs
sections.content:
The cost is the same shape Stalin's is: **no separate compilation** (the whole program must be available), **slow compilation** for large programs (whole-program analyses scale poorly — 100 KLOC SML programs commonly take minutes to compile), and **incompatibility with `eval` / dynamic loading**. MLton's `.mlb` (ML Basis) files are the project structure; large MLton programs partition into compilation units only at the build-system level, not at the language level.

Production: the **HOL4** theorem prover compiles to MLton for production performance; **Mythryl** (a syntactic variant of SML) ships on MLton; **MaPLe** parallel SML extension (PLDI 2021) is an MLton fork; **Roc's compiler** inherits MLton's defunctionalisation and contification techniques. Status (as of 2026-04): MLton is actively maintained but moves slowly; the codebase is roughly 200 KLOC of SML and stands as the longest-running production-grade whole-program functional-language compiler.

sources.label: Cejtin et al. — Flow-Directed Closure Conversion (POPL 2000)
